title: Writing Data and Saving Output
description: Export data to CSV, Excel, RDS, and save your workspace.

slides.title: Writing CSV Files 💾
slides.content:
To export a data frame to CSV:

Base R: `write.csv(df, "file.csv", row.names = FALSE)`

readr: `write_csv(df, "file.csv")` (no row names by default)

```r
write.csv(mtcars, "mtcars.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write_csv(mtcars, "mtcars.csv")
```

slides.title: write.csv Example
slides.content: Run this to write a CSV (it will appear in your working directory):
slides.codeSnippet:
write.csv(mtcars, "mtcars_test.csv", row.names = FALSE)
print("File written. Check your working directory.")

quizData.question: What does `row.names = FALSE` do in `write.csv()`?

options.text: Prevents row names from being written as a column
Writes row names as the first column

quizData.explanation: By default, row names become an unnamed first column; setting to FALSE avoids that.

slides.title: Writing Excel Files
slides.content:
Use the `writexl` package (simple) or `openxlsx` for more control.

```r
install.packages("writexl")
library(writexl)
write_xlsx(df, "file.xlsx")
```

slides.title: writexl Example
slides.codeSnippet:
# library(writexl)
# write_xlsx(mtcars, "mtcars.xlsx")
print("write_xlsx creates an Excel file.")

slides.title: Saving R Objects
slides.content:
- `saveRDS(obj, "file.rds")` – saves a single object; read with `readRDS()`.
- `save(obj1, obj2, file = "data.RData")` – saves multiple objects; load with `load("data.RData")`.
- `save.image("workspace.RData")` – saves all objects in the workspace.

slides.title: saveRDS Example
slides.codeSnippet:
my_list <- list(a = 1:5, b = "hello")
saveRDS(my_list, "my_list.rds")
# To read it back:
new_list <- readRDS("my_list.rds")
print(new_list)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Use RDS for saving intermediate results; it's faster than CSV and preserves data types (factors, dates, etc.).
